snippets.title: StandardScaler by hand and with sklearn
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X_train = np.array([[1.0, 1000.0], [2.0, 2000.0], [3.0, 3000.0]])
X_test = np.array([[2.5, 2500.0]])

# Manual (per column)
mu = X_train.mean(axis=0)
sigma = X_train.std(axis=0, ddof=1)
X_train_z = (X_train - mu) / sigma
X_test_z = (X_test - mu) / sigma

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_sk = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_sk = scaler.transform(X_test)
print("train mean ~0:", X_train_sk.mean(axis=0).round(3))
print("test uses train stats:", X_test_sk.round(3))
snippets.explanation: Fit location and scale on training rows only; transform test with the same μ and σ so holdout data never influences preprocessing.

snippets.title: MinMaxScaler to [0, 1]
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

X_train = np.array([[0.0], [10.0], [20.0], [100.0]])  # 100 is an outlier
X_test = np.array([[50.0]])

mm = MinMaxScaler()
X_train_mm = mm.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_mm = mm.transform(X_test)
print("train min/max:", X_train_mm.min(), X_train_mm.max())
print("test 50 maps to:", X_test_mm[0, 0].round(3))
snippets.explanation: The train outlier 100 sets max=100, so 50 maps to 0.5; typical points 0–20 are compressed into a tiny slice of [0, 1].

snippets.title: Pipeline: scaler + k-NN (no leakage)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
import numpy as np

X = np.column_stack([
    np.random.default_rng(0).integers(20, 60, 200),      # age
    np.random.default_rng(1).normal(80, 30, 200),        # spend
])
y = (X[:, 1] > 85).astype(int)

pipe = Pipeline([
    ("scale", StandardScaler()),
    ("knn", KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)),
])
print("CV accuracy:", cross_val_score(pipe, X, y, cv=5).mean().round(3))
snippets.explanation: The scaler refits inside each CV fold on training folds only—wrap scaler and model together instead of scaling the full matrix once.

snippets.title: Compare unscaled vs scaled k-means inertia
snippets.code:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X = pd.DataFrame({"sessions": [2, 4, 40, 3, 38],
                  "spend": [10, 20, 900, 15, 850]})

km_raw = KMeans(n_clusters=2, random_state=0, n_init=10).fit(X)
X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
km_scaled = KMeans(n_clusters=2, random_state=0, n_init=10).fit(X_scaled)

print("labels raw:", km_raw.labels_)
print("labels scaled:", km_scaled.labels_)
snippets.explanation: Raw clustering follows spend; scaled clustering can separate low/high engagement across both sessions and spend.
